blocks.text: David Mcgee, General Manager, {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}
Re: Retained Engagement for Executive Compensation Benchmarking, Hicks-Castillo
Dear Mr. Mcgee:

This letter sets out the terms on which Northgate Talent Partners LLC will conduct an executive compensation benchmarking study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. When you have signed it and returned a copy, this letter is the agreement between us. It is short by design. If anything in it does not match what we discussed, tell me before you sign rather than after.
Scope of Services

We will benchmark the compensation of your senior leadership roles against a defined peer group and return a set of ranges you can defend to your board. The study covers base salary, annual incentive, and long-term or deferred incentive where it exists in the peer set, together with the pay mix at each level. We report on positions, not on people. Our findings describe the market for a seat, and where we discuss an incumbent's placement against a range we will do so by role and by band rather than by name. That is our practice everywhere and it is not negotiable here, because a benchmarking study that circulates with names attached stops being a planning document and becomes a personnel file.

items: Construction of a peer group with you, screened on revenue, headcount, and geography, and agreed in writing before any data is pulled.
Role matching for each in-scope position, mapping your titles to the market roles the survey data actually measures.
Collection and normalization of market data from licensed survey sources and from our own engagement records, aged to a common effective date.
Ranges at the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles for base salary, target total cash, and total direct compensation, by role and by band.
A written analysis of where your present structure sits against those ranges, with the outliers called out and explained rather than listed.
A presentation of the findings to you and to whichever audience you designate, with a second sitting available at your request.

blocks.text: Our Approach

The work that determines whether a benchmarking study is worth anything happens in the first six weeks, not the last. Peer group selection and role matching are where these studies go wrong, and they go wrong quietly: the numbers come back looking authoritative, and nobody notices that half the matched roles carry a scope your organization does not have. We will spend more time with you on those two steps than you probably expect, and we will not move past either one without your agreement in writing.

After that the work is mostly arithmetic and judgment about aging and blending sources. We will show you our sources and our weights. You should be able to take any number we hand you and rebuild it yourself, and if you cannot, we have not done the job.
Engagement Team

Sandra Fuentes, our Principal, will lead the engagement and is your day-to-day contact. She sets the standard in this firm for how information about people is handled, and she will be the one who tells you no if a request would put us over that line. David Weiss, Research Associate, will build the peer group and run the data collection, and you will see his name on most of what reaches you between meetings. I will stay close to the work and will attend the peer group review and the findings presentation. If at any point the engagement is not going the way you expected, call me directly rather than waiting for a status report to tell you. The people named here are the people who will do the work. We do not staff a pitch with one team and deliver with another, and Hicks-Castillo will not be handed to anyone you have not met.
Fee and Start Date

Our fee for the engagement as scoped above is {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}}. Work begins {{fact:f:E-2023-001.start}}, and we expect the study to run approximately eight months from that point through the findings presentation. The fee is fixed. It does not vary with the hours we spend, and it does not vary if the work proves harder than we thought.
Fees and Payment

The fee is billed in three installments: one third on execution of this letter, one third on delivery of the agreed peer group and role match, and one third on delivery of the written findings. Invoices are due thirty days from date of issue. Out-of-pocket expenses, which for a study of this kind means survey data licenses and any travel you ask us to make, are billed at cost and will not exceed five percent of the fee without your prior written approval. If you expand the scope, we will price the addition and get your approval in writing before we start it. We do not send surprise invoices.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from the start date set out above through delivery of the findings, unless one of us ends it sooner. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice. If you terminate, you owe the installments already earned plus expenses incurred to that point and nothing further. If we terminate, we will refund any portion of the fee attributable to work not performed, and we will hand over the peer group, the role match, and whatever analysis exists in usable form. Neither of us should have to litigate to walk away from a study.
Confidentiality

Everything {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}} gives us about your organization, your pay structure, and your people is confidential, and we will use it only for this engagement. We will not disclose that we are working for you without your permission. Our work product is yours to use inside your organization and with your board and your advisors. It is not for publication or for distribution to third parties without our consent, because the survey licenses underlying it do not permit that. Our obligations here survive the end of the engagement, and so does that one of yours. At close-out we will return or destroy the compensation data you provide to us and confirm to you that we have done so.
Limitation of Liability

Our aggregate liability to {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}} arising out of this engagement is limited to the fee paid. We are giving you market data and our judgment about it. We are not giving you legal, tax, or accounting advice, and the decision about what to pay anyone remains yours. This limitation does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct, and we are not asking it to.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the law of the state in which Northgate Talent Partners LLC maintains its principal place of business, without regard to its conflict of laws rules. It is the entire agreement between us on this subject and it replaces anything said before it. It may be amended only in writing signed by both of us.
If this reflects our understanding, please sign both copies and return one to me. We are ready to begin on the date set out above.
Very truly yours,
To: Engagement team. From: David Weiss, Research. Re: Kickoff notes, Compensation Benchmarking for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}.

This is David, writing up what came out of the kickoff so we all carry the same version of it. We start formally on {{fact:f:E-2023-001.start}}, and the letter gives us about eight months, which sounds generous right up until you remember that the peer group has to be agreed in writing before we are allowed to pull a single number. The clock that matters is the one on the front end.

The objective in the client's own words: David Mcgee wants ranges he can take to his board without being asked a question he cannot answer. He was blunt about why. He has been handed benchmarking decks before that came apart the moment somebody asked where a number came from. So everything we build has to be reconstructable from the source up, and the source log is not an afterthought this time. Sandra Fuentes made the same point back to him from the other direction: what Hicks-Castillo actually needs is not a study, it is an argument they can still defend six months after we hand it over.
Workstreams

items: Peer group construction. I own the first cut, screened on revenue, headcount, and geography. Sandra reviews before anything goes to the client. The deliverable is a screened list with the rationale written next to each company, not just the list.

Role matching. Heather Meyer is taking the title-to-market-role mapping for the in-scope seats. This is the piece that quietly sinks these studies, so we are doing it twice, independently, and reconciling the differences rather than one of us marking the other's homework.

Data collection and normalization. Licensed survey sources first, then our own engagement records where they add anything real. Everything ages to one common effective date and the aging assumption gets written down somewhere the client can see it.
Analysis and ranges. 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles for base, target total cash, and total direct, by role and by band.

Findings and presentation. Written analysis first, deck second. Sandra was firm that we do not build slides before we know what we think, and having now watched it go the other way once, I am not going to argue.

blocks.text: Ground rules on names

One thing I want written down, mostly because I got it wrong in a draft already and would rather the correction live somewhere everyone can see it than be repeated privately to the next person. We describe incumbents by seat, not by name. The current head of operations, not the person's name, in anything that leaves the room, and honestly in most of what stays in it. Sandra Fuentes caught this in my notes from the intro call and she was right. The reason is not squeamishness. The moment a name lands next to a percentile in a document, the document turns into something else, and the client cannot circulate it internally without causing a problem he did not ask for. Heather Meyer already writes this way without having been told, which is faintly annoying for those of us who had to be.
Next steps

items: I get the draft peer screen to Sandra by the end of next week, with the rationale attached per company.
Heather and I run the role match independently and reconcile the week after.
Sandra takes the agreed peer group and role match to David Mcgee for written sign-off. Nothing gets pulled before that comes back.
I stand up the source log now rather than at the end, so the audit trail builds itself as we go instead of getting reconstructed under time pressure in month seven.
Somebody who is not me should own the calendar for the client checkpoints. Volunteering Heather Meyer purely because she is better at it than I am, and she should feel free to hand it straight back.

blocks.text: That is everything I took down. If I have misremembered a commitment, correct me now while it is cheap. Sandra, the peer screen is the one I most want your eyes on early, because if the peer set is wrong then nothing downstream can save it and we will not find out until the end.
Working session on the compensation benchmarking study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. These minutes were taken by Sandra Fuentes and circulate to the attendees below and to the engagement file.
Attendees

items: Sandra Fuentes, Principal, Northgate Talent Partners LLC (chair)
David Weiss, Research Associate, Northgate Talent Partners LLC
David Mcgee, General Manager, Hicks-Castillo

blocks.text: Discussion

David Weiss reported that collection is complete for the agreed peer group and that normalization is finished for base salary and annual incentive across all in-scope roles. Long-term incentive remains partial. Several of the peer companies are privately held and disclose nothing usable, and for those we are relying on survey participation rather than filings. He was candid that this leaves the long-term incentive ranges thinner than the cash ranges. I want that stated plainly in the findings rather than smoothed into a footnote. A range built on the full peer set and a range built on a handful of observations are not the same object, and the client is entitled to know which one he is looking at.

The longer discussion was about role matching at the operations level. The seat as the client defines it carries both plant responsibility and a commercial line, and the market roles the survey data measures generally separate the two. Mr. Mcgee's position is that the combined scope is deliberate and is not going to change, so a match to either single-scope role understates the seat. My view is that he is right about the scope and that the honest treatment is a blended match, disclosed as a blend, with both component ranges shown alongside it so the board can see the construction rather than take our word for it. We agreed on that approach. David Weiss will rebuild the match on that basis.

I restated our practice on names, at Mr. Mcgee's prompting rather than at my own, which I note with some appreciation. The written findings will describe seats and bands. Where an incumbent sits against a range, that placement is reported by role. If Hicks-Castillo wants a name-level view for its compensation committee, we will prepare that as a separate and short document with its own distribution list, and it will not be stapled to the study. He agreed, and said his concern was the mirror image of ours: he does not want the study circulating with names in it either.

He then raised what happens if the ranges come back showing the company materially below market at two levels. He does not want a study that reads as a demand for money the business does not have. I told him what I would tell anyone. We report what the market pays. What to do about it is his decision and his board's. We can and will show the cost of closing to various points in the range so that the choice is priced rather than abstract, but we are not going to shade the ranges to make the answer comfortable. He took that well, and said he would rather be told early than managed.
Action items

rows: Rebuild the operations role match as a disclosed blend, retaining both component ranges
Circulate the aging and weighting assumptions in writing for client review
Draft the cost-to-close exhibit at three points in the range
Confirm scope and distribution of the separate committee document
Find a defensible source for long-term incentive at the private peers, or state the limitation
Review and comment on the draft findings outline

blocks.text: Corrections to these minutes should reach me within the week; after that they stand as the record. I will add, because it is true and because David Weiss will not write it about himself, that the normalization behind this session came back faster and cleaner than the engagement letter promised, and Mr. Mcgee said as much himself before we adjourned.
Status report, compensation benchmarking for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Prepared by David Weiss, Research Associate. Reviewed by Sandra Fuentes, Principal.
Where we are

We are at roughly the midpoint and the shape of the answer is starting to show. The peer group is agreed and signed off. The role match is agreed, including the blended treatment for the operations seat that we settled on at the last working session. The cash data is collected and normalized to a common effective date. What remains is the long-term incentive gap at the privately held peers, the analysis itself, and the writing. We are on the eight-month plan set out in the engagement letter, with the one caveat in the risks section below.

One thing worth saying plainly at the midpoint, because it is the part you are paying for and also the part that is hardest to see from outside: the peer group and the role match are finished, and they are finished properly. That sounds like a modest claim. It is not. Those two decisions determine every number that comes after them, and they are the two that are easiest to rush, because nothing visible happens while you argue about them and the calendar keeps moving anyway. We argued about them. Everything you receive in the back half of this engagement inherits that work, and had we hurried it there would be no way to tell from the output that anything had gone wrong.

On money: the engagement is fixed-fee at {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}}, and nothing we have done or plan to do changes that number. The second installment came due on delivery of the peer group and role match and has been invoiced. Expenses to date are survey licenses only, well inside the five percent cap the letter sets, and there has been no travel to bill. If the scope grows we will price it and ask before we start. As of today there is nothing in that column.
Completed this period

items: Peer group finalized and signed off, with the written rationale for each company retained in the source log rather than in somebody's memory.
Role match completed for every in-scope seat, including the blended construction for operations, with both component ranges preserved so the blend can be taken apart by anyone who wants to.
Base salary and target total cash normalized across the full peer set and aged to a single effective date. The aging and weighting assumptions went to you in writing and came back without objection.
Cost-to-close exhibit drafted at three points in the range, so the eventual decision arrives priced rather than as a direction.
Source log built and current. Every number that will appear in the findings traces to a source, an effective date, and an aging factor.

blocks.text: Upcoming work

items: Long-term incentive: finish the survey-participation approach for the private peers, or state the limitation in the findings and put the cash-only view next to it. Sandra Fuentes will decide which, and she will not let it go out looking more solid than it is.
Full analysis and range construction at the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles, by role and by band.
Written findings, then the presentation. In that order, deliberately.
A working session at roughly the three-quarter mark to walk the draft findings with you before anything is finalized. Nothing in this study should surprise you at the end.

blocks.text: Risks I would rather flag now than later

items: Long-term incentive coverage. The private peers do not disclose, and survey participation is thinner at this revenue size than I assumed when I scoped the collection. That one is on me. Mitigation: we show the ranges we can defend and we are explicit about which ones rest on a handful of observations. I would rather hand you a narrow claim you can use than a wide one you cannot.

The operations blend. It is the right answer and it is also the number most likely to be challenged, precisely because it is constructed rather than observed. Mitigation: both component ranges travel with it and the construction is shown, not asserted.

Below-market findings at the middle levels. Early indications point that way at two of them. This is not a risk to the study, it is a risk to the conversation the study starts, and the cost-to-close exhibit exists so that conversation has numbers in it rather than adjectives.

My own drafting. Sandra Fuentes has already sent one section back for describing a seat specifically enough that it stopped being anonymous in a company the size of Hicks-Castillo. She was right, and it was not a close call. Everything now goes through her before it reaches you, which is slower and is the correct trade.

blocks.text: Nothing here needs a decision from you this week. If you want the cost-to-close exhibit early in draft, I can send it. It is rough but it is directionally right, and some clients would rather see the shape sooner than see it polished later. Say the word and it goes out.
Working session on the draft findings for the compensation benchmarking study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Minutes taken by Sandra Fuentes.
Present

items: Sandra Fuentes, Principal, Northgate Talent Partners LLC
David Weiss, Research Associate, Northgate Talent Partners LLC
David Mcgee, General Manager, Hicks-Castillo

blocks.text: Discussion

We walked the draft findings end to end. David Weiss presented and I interrupted rather more than was strictly necessary. The headline is what we signaled at the midpoint and it has not moved: the company sits at or slightly above market on base salary at the top of the house, and materially below market on target total cash at the two levels beneath it. The gap is not in salary. It is in the annual incentive design, which pays out on a formula the peer set largely abandoned some years ago. That is a considerably more useful finding than a flat statement that the company underpays, because it points at a mechanism that can be fixed rather than at a number that can only be argued with.

Mr. Mcgee's first question was the right one, which was how confident we are. David Weiss took him through the source log live, pulled two figures apart at his request, and showed the aging factor applied to each. The exercise took the better part of half an hour and was worth every minute of it. Mr. Mcgee said afterward that he had never been able to do that with a benchmarking study before. I record it here because it is the return on building the log in the first month instead of the seventh, and the next person tempted to defer it should read this paragraph.

The long-term incentive section remains the weakest part of the study, and we said so out loud rather than waiting for him to find it. For the privately held peers we have survey participation and nothing better, and the resulting range is thin. My instruction to David Weiss stands: show it, label it, and set the cash-only view beside it so the reader can choose which to lean on. Mr. Mcgee accepted this without argument. His view is that his board will discount a thin range appropriately if we tell them it is thin, and will never forgive us for not saying so.

On distribution and on names. The findings describe seats and bands throughout, and I confirmed that the current draft contains no incumbent names anywhere in it. The separate committee document is scoped, short, and goes only to the compensation committee under Mr. Mcgee's own cover. He asked whether Hicks-Castillo may share the study with its outside counsel. The answer is yes, subject to the same internal-use limitation the letter carries, and I will confirm that in writing so that neither of us has to rely on a memory of a meeting.
Actions agreed

items: David Weiss: rework the annual incentive section to lead with the design finding rather than the gap number. Draft to me before it circulates.
David Weiss: finalize the long-term incentive treatment as labeled-and-thin, with the cash-only view alongside.
Sandra Fuentes: confirm the outside counsel sharing permission in writing.
Sandra Fuentes: send the compensation committee document scope for Mr. Mcgee's approval before it is drafted.
David Mcgee: identify the audience for the findings presentation, and say whether the committee sitting is the same session or a separate one.
David Weiss: assemble the exhibits in presentation order once the written findings are locked, and not before.

blocks.text: Corrections to me within the week. I will say, since the record ought to carry it, that David Weiss handled being audited live on his own numbers with more composure than most people at twice his tenure would have managed, and the study is better than it would have been had we not spent the front end the way we did.
Final status report, compensation benchmarking for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Prepared by David Weiss, Research Associate. Reviewed by Sandra Fuentes, Principal.
Summary

This is the last status report of the engagement. The written findings are delivered, the presentation is delivered, and the compensation committee document went out under your cover as agreed. What is left is the second sitting the engagement letter entitles you to, if you want it, and the close-out of the file. The study came in on the eight-month plan without an extension, which I would like to attribute to good management and should probably attribute to the fact that we refused to move past the peer group until it was actually agreed.

The short version of what we found, for the file and for anyone who reads this report without the findings sitting next to it: the pay problem here was never the salary line. It is the annual incentive design, and it can be fixed without a structural increase to fixed cost. That is a considerably better answer than the one I expected to be writing when we scoped this in the spring, and it is that answer because the peer group was right, not because anyone got clever at the end. Sandra Fuentes would want me to add that a study reaching the opposite conclusion would have been just as good a study. We do not get to pick the finding. We only get to pick whether it holds up.
Delivered this period

items: Written findings, complete: ranges at the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles for base salary, target total cash, and total direct compensation, by role and by band, across the agreed peer set.

The annual incentive design finding, reworked to lead with the mechanism rather than the gap. That was Sandra Fuentes's call and it was the right one; it has been the part of the study your people actually ask about.
Long-term incentive ranges, shown and labeled as thin where the private peers do not disclose, with the cash-only view set beside them.
Cost-to-close exhibit at three points in the range, so the board conversation has priced options in front of it rather than a direction.
Source log, delivered with the findings rather than held back. Every figure traces to a source, an effective date, and an aging factor. You can rebuild any number in the study without us in the room.
Findings presentation delivered to the audience you designated, and the separate committee document delivered under your cover with its own distribution list.

blocks.text: What is left

items: The optional second sitting. The engagement letter gives you one at your request and we have not heard whether you want it. Sandra Fuentes and I are both available.

File close-out on our side, which includes returning or destroying the raw compensation data you provided, per the confidentiality section. I will send you confirmation when it is done rather than leaving you to wonder.
The final invoice, which follows delivery of the written findings and is with our billing now.

blocks.text: Budget

The engagement was fixed-fee at {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}} and closed at that number. No change orders, no scope additions, no line items you have not seen before. Expenses were survey licenses only and landed well inside the five percent cap; there was no travel to bill. The third and final installment is the last thing you will see from us on this engagement.
Risks

items: Shelf life. These ranges are aged to a single effective date, and the market at the two levels where you are behind has been moving. The study is good for planning through your next cycle and I would not lean on it much past that. That is not a defect. It is what a point-in-time benchmark is, and anyone who tells you otherwise is selling you a longer study.

The thin long-term incentive ranges. Still thin, still labeled. If your board pushes on them, the honest answer is the one already in the document: the private peers do not disclose, and we did not pretend otherwise.

The conversation, not the study. The findings say Hicks-Castillo is materially below market on target total cash at two levels. That is now a decision rather than a question, and decisions have a way of stalling until the next cycle makes them worse. The cost-to-close exhibit exists so that this one does not have to. Not our risk to carry, but I would be doing the job badly if I closed the file without naming it.

blocks.text: It has been a good engagement to work on, and I am not saying that only because it is the last page of a status report. If you want the second sitting, say so and we will schedule it. If you would rather we close the file, tell me that too and I will get you the data destruction confirmation and get out of your inbox.
